design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate themed levels using varied Mask abilities (punch, mallet, horn, guns, tornado, invisibility, dash) to defeat film-inspired bosses and reach the final confrontation with Dorian
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource management of Morph power meter (special ability fuel) balanced against enemy encounters; visual maze navigation complexity creating spatial disorientation
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Embody the cartoon chaos of The Mask film character, wielding absurd slapstick weapons and abilities adapted from iconic movie scenes
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Game prioritizes faithful recreation of film's humor and cartoony visual style (comic-book aesthetic over film photorealism) and ability variety over traditional platformer precision; accepts convoluted level design as intentional maze-exploration consequence

difficulty.lives_system: Health depletion reverts player to civilian Ipkiss form (pajamas), representing depowered state rather than traditional death
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Power-up collection for Morph meter replenishment creates risk/reward in exploration; ability variety provides multiple approaches to encounters

progression.upgrade_system: Morph power meter management through power-up collection; meter slowly replenishes passively to smaller amount (similar to Earthworm Jim ammo system)
progression.unlock_mechanism: Sequential level access: Ipkiss apartment → high-rent district → outside → bank (interior/exterior) → park → prison → nightclub final boss confrontation
replayability_hooks: Secret ways to use backgrounds for alternate navigation paths
Hidden power-up locations affecting Morph availability
Multiple ability approaches to enemy encounters

world_building_techniques: Character-themed boss encounters matching film (Mrs. Peenman, Irv and Burt Ripley, Dorian)
Iconic ability recreation from film scenes (mallet, tornado, pocket guns, living horn)
Location progression mirroring film geography (apartment, nightclub, bank, prison)
16-bit Cameron Diaz representation in ending dance sequence

boss_design.philosophy: Boss encounters feature major characters from The Mask film, serving as environmental/narrative landmarks within level progression rather than isolated skill tests
notable_bosses: Irv and Burt Ripley (careless repairmen duo)
Dorian Tyrell (final boss, also wearing the mask, uses massive guns)

faction_indicators: Civilian form (Ipkiss pajamas) vs. Masked form (powered, cartoonish)
Gang presence under Dorian's control (henchmen encounters)
danger_signals: Prison level with stereotypically uniformed inmates
Health depletion reverting player to vulnerable civilian state
environmental_storytelling: Progression through Edge City locations reveals nightclub economy (wealthy patrons, mob operation)
Apartment setting establishes Stanley Ipkiss's mundane starting point

sources_used: wikipedia: The Mask (video game) - Wikipedia article covering gameplay mechanics, story, development, reception
